contest.title: European Championship 2025 - Online Mirror (Unrated, ICPC Rules, Teams Preferred)

sources.notes: Statement and constraints: construct a grid of parks/built-up cells with exactly k rectangular walks, for 0 <= k <= 4.194e12 and h,w <= 2025.
Official Codeforces EUC editorial entry; used to identify the tutorial source for problem B.
Official ICPC Europe Championship solutions PDF; used for the full construction with an almost-full n by n+1 rectangle, corner deletions, and residual segments.
Chinese implementation notes for CF2068B; used to cross-check the diagonal top-left deletion order and the conversion of the remaining value into bottom-row/right-column segments.

Re-derived the marginal-count invariant: top-left corner deletions destroy only rectangles whose top-left corner is the deleted cell, while separated added segments contribute independent triangular-number multiples.

reference_candidates.notes: Candidate Luogu solution page; not moved into sources because the worker encountered an authorization/login barrier.

annotation.summary: Given k, output a grid with at most 2025 rows and columns whose park cells contain exactly k axis-aligned rectangular walks, where only the boundary of each rectangle must be all parks.
annotation.constraints: 0 <= k <= 4.194e12; h,w <= 2025. Counts are around C(2025,2)^2, so all arithmetic must be 64-bit, and the construction must fit in one bounded grid rather than relying on many disconnected large components.
annotation.core_idea: A full H by W all-park block contributes C(H,2)*C(W,2) walks. For large k, choose n <= 2023 and start from a full n by (n+1) block with F(n)=C(n,2)*C(n+1,2) close to k. Delete cells from the top-left corner by increasing anti-diagonals until the remaining count x gives d=k-x in the target range n(n-1) <= d < 2n(n-1). The key invariant is that removing cell (r,c) from this top-left Young-diagram corner newly destroys exactly the rectangles whose top-left boundary corner is (r,c), so the loss is (n-r)*(n+1-c). Because gcd(n,n+1)=1 and d is large enough, write d as p*n + q*(n+1). Decompose p and q into triangular numbers C(len,2). A contiguous segment of len new cells in the extra bottom row under untouched columns adds C(len,2)*n walks, and a segment of len new cells in the extra right column beside untouched rows adds C(len,2)*(n+1) walks. Gaps between segments and an empty bottom-right corner prevent unintended cross-segment rectangles. Small k can be handled by isolated 2 by 2 blocks or an easier direct fill.
annotation.complexity: O(2025^2) time and memory to materialize the grid; the arithmetic/planning part is O(number of removed cells + number of residual segments) and uses 64-bit integers.
tricks: count a full park block as C(rows,2)*C(cols,2)
delete a top-left Young-diagram corner so each removed cell has a known marginal loss
represent the residual as p*n + q*(n+1) using consecutive coprime coefficients
decompose p and q into triangular numbers C(len,2)
separate added segments by dots to avoid unintended rectangles

solution_variants.name: near-full-rectangle-residual-segments
solution_variants.summary: For large k, start with an n by (n+1) all-park rectangle, delete a controlled top-left corner to leave a residual d in a representable range, then realize d by bottom-row and right-column separated segments whose contributions are triangular-number multiples of n and n+1.

solution_variants.name: small-k-separated-blocks
solution_variants.summary: For sufficiently small k, place isolated 2 by 2 all-park blocks separated by empty rows and columns; each block contributes exactly one rectangular walk and no cross-block rectangle is possible.

tags.evidence: The problem asks for an explicit grid, and the solution is an exact construction of a park pattern with a prescribed rectangle count.
The construction is driven by combinatorial rectangle counts C(H,2)*C(W,2) and by exact marginal counts for deleting or adding cells.
The residual coefficients p and q are represented as sums of triangular numbers C(len,2), and each triangular term is materialized as one independent segment.
After corner deletion, the remaining value d is expressed as p*n + q*(n+1) using gcd(n,n+1)=1 and modular residue/Frobenius-style representability.
The implementation can greedily remove cells until the residual is in range and greedily decompose p and q into the largest triangular numbers that fit.
Correctness depends on knowing the exact contribution change of each removed corner cell and each added segment, then summing those independent contributions.
